title: Teaching Thinking Models to Reason with Tools: A Full-Pipeline Recipe for Tool-Integrated Reasoning
summary: Tool-integrated reasoning (TIR) offers a direct way to extend thinking models beyond the limits of text-only reasoning. Paradoxically, we observe that tool-enabled evaluation can degrade reasoning performance even when the strong thinking models make almost no actual tool calls. In this paper, we investigate how to inject natural tool-use behavior into a strong thinking model without sacrificing its no-tool reasoning ability, and present a comprehensive TIR recipe. We highlight that (i) the effectiveness of TIR supervised fine-tuning (SFT) hinges on the learnability of teacher trajectories, which should prioritize problems inherently suited for tool-augmented solutions; (ii) controlling the proportion of tool-use trajectories could mitigate the catastrophic forgetting of text-only reasoning capacity; (iii) optimizing for pass@k and response length instead of training loss could maximize TIR SFT gains while preserving headroom for reinforcement learning (RL) exploration; (iv) a stable RL with verifiable rewards (RLVR) stage, built upon suitable SFT initialization and explicit safeguards against mode collapse, provides a simple yet remarkably effective solution. When applied to Qwen3 thinking models at 4B and 30B scales, our recipe yields models that achieve state-of-the-art performance in a wide range of benchmarks among open-source models, such as 96.7% and 99.2% on AIME 2025 for 4B and 30B, respectively.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a practical recipe to train tool-integrated reasoning (TIR) models that gain strong tool-use abilities without losing text-only reasoning performance, achieving SOTA results on math benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Although tool-integrated reasoning should in principle extend a model’s capabilities, the authors find that enabling tools in evaluation can actually hurt reasoning, especially when strong models rarely call tools. This indicates current training methods do not produce natural, beneficial tool-use behavior and often damage baseline text-only reasoning. The motivation is to understand why this happens and to design a training protocol that makes models use tools when they truly help, while preserving or improving pure reasoning skills.
AI.method: They analyze the training dynamics of TIR and then propose a multi-part recipe: (i) design supervised fine-tuning (SFT) data where teacher trajectories are learnable and focus on problems truly suited for tool-augmented solutions; (ii) control the ratio of tool-use vs pure text trajectories to avoid catastrophic forgetting of text-only reasoning; (iii) during SFT, optimize model selection using pass@k and response length instead of just training loss to better capture actual reasoning quality and leave room for later RL improvements; (iv) add a reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) stage, initialized from the well-tuned SFT model and equipped with safeguards against mode collapse, to further refine tool usage. They implement this on Qwen3 thinking models at different scales.
AI.result: Using this recipe on Qwen3 thinking models of 4B and 30B parameters, they obtain tool-integrated models that substantially outperform prior open-source reasoning models on many benchmarks. Notably, they reach 96.7% and 99.2% accuracy on AIME 2025 for the 4B and 30B models, respectively, which are state-of-the-art among open-source models. The models exhibit strong tool-use behavior without significant degradation in text-only reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Effective TIR training is not just about adding tools but about curating data and objectives so that the model learns when and how tools help. By carefully constructing tool-suited SFT trajectories, balancing tool and non-tool data, using evaluation metrics aligned with reasoning quality, and applying a stable RLVR stage with safeguards, one can build models that gain powerful tool-augmented reasoning while preserving or enhancing their base reasoning. The demonstrated gains on Qwen3 suggest this recipe is a strong and practical path forward for open-source TIR systems.

title: PRAISE: Prefix-Based Rollout Reuse in Agentic Search Training
summary: In agentic search, large language models (LLMs) are trained to perform multi-turn retrieval and reasoning for complex tasks such as multi-hop question answering (QA). However, current search-based Reinforcement Learning (RL) methods suffer from two core limitations: expensive long-horizon rollouts are under-utilized during training, and supervision is typically available only at the final answer, resulting in severe reward sparsity. We present Prefix-based Rollout reuse for Agentic search with Intermediate Step rEwards (PRAISE), a framework for improving both data efficiency and credit assignment in agentic search training. Given a complete search trajectory, PRAISE extracts prefix states at different search turns, elicits intermediate answers from them, and uses these prefixes both to construct additional training trajectories and to derive step-level rewards from performance differences across prefixes. Our method uses a single shared model for both search policy learning and prefix answer evaluation, enabling joint optimization without extra human annotations or a separate reward model. Experiments on multi-hop QA benchmarks show that PRAISE consistently improves performance over strong baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PRAISE, a framework that improves agentic search training for LLMs by reusing rollout prefixes and generating intermediate step rewards, leading to better data efficiency and credit assignment in multi-hop QA tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing search-based RL methods for agentic LLMs are inefficient because (1) long-horizon rollouts are not fully exploited during training, and (2) rewards are only given at the final answer, causing severe reward sparsity and poor credit assignment. The authors aim to improve how RL leverages search trajectories and to provide richer supervision signals during multi-turn reasoning and retrieval.
AI.method: PRAISE takes complete agentic search trajectories and decomposes them into prefixes at different search turns. For each prefix, the model is prompted to produce an intermediate answer. These prefixes are then (a) reused as additional training trajectories and (b) compared to compute step-level rewards based on changes in performance between prefixes. A single shared LLM is used both as the search policy and as the evaluator of intermediate answers, enabling joint optimization without extra human labels or a separate reward model.
AI.result: On multi-hop QA benchmarks, models trained with PRAISE achieve consistently better performance than strong existing search-based RL baselines, indicating improvements in both data efficiency and credit assignment quality for agentic search.
AI.conclusion: Reusing rollout prefixes and generating intermediate rewards from them is an effective way to address reward sparsity and under-utilization of long trajectories in agentic search. PRAISE demonstrates that a single shared LLM can jointly learn search policies and evaluate intermediate reasoning steps without additional supervision, yielding superior performance on complex multi-hop QA tasks.
